findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile where `run_e2_benchmark.emit_baseline` is tested: either (a) add `test_e2_counting.py`'s scope in Scope to include `emit_baseline` enforcement, or (b) move the AC6 prove command to a separate test file (e.g., `test_e2_benchmark.py`) and update Scope accordingly.
evidence: Scope: 'tests/unit/experiments/test_e2_counting.py — CI-collectable golden test for BOTH `classify_pair` and `run_validity` (synthetic PASS/BLOCK/INDETERMINATE dicts incl. a >20%-INDETERMINATE fixture; no LLM)'
AC6: 'An integration test drives `emit_baseline` with a synthetic >20%-INDETERMINATE per-class fixture … Prove: `.venv/bin/python -m pytest tests/unit/experiments/test_e2_counting.py -k enforce -q`'
emit_baseline is defined as living in run_e2_benchmark.py, not e2_counting.py
findings.finding: AC6's prove command points to `test_e2_counting.py` for testing `run_e2_benchmark.emit_baseline`, but the Scope section defines `test_e2_counting.py` as covering only `e2_counting.classify_pair` and `e2_counting.run_validity`. `emit_baseline` is a function in `run_e2_benchmark.py`, not `e2_counting.py`. An implementer reading Scope will not add `emit_baseline` tests to `test_e2_counting.py`; an implementer reading AC6 will, but then the Scope's description of `test_e2_counting.py` is wrong.
findings.location: Scope (test_e2_counting.py entry) vs AC6 prove command
scenarios: Implementer writes test_e2_counting.py covering only classify_pair/run_validity per Scope, AC6 prove command then fails the enforce filter
Implementer puts emit_baseline test in test_e2_counting.py per AC6, but the test now imports from run_e2_benchmark.py, making the test file's name and scope description misleading
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `tests/unit/experiments/test_e2_benchmark.py` entry to Scope covering the `emit_baseline` seam test, and update AC6's prove command to point to that file. Keep `test_e2_counting.py` scoped to the two pure helpers as Scope describes.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the AC whether `emit_baseline` integration tests live in `test_e2_counting.py` (requiring a cross-module import) or a separate `test_e2_benchmark.py`, and update the prove command accordingly — or add a `--co -q` dry-run assertion that confirms at least one `enforce`-marked test is collected.
evidence: Scope: 'e2_counting.py — the two pure helpers classify_pair and run_validity… imported by the harness and by the unit test'
Scope: 'run_e2_benchmark.py — … emit_baseline(per_class_verdicts, out_path) -> int that calls run_validity'
AC 6: '.venv/bin/python -m pytest tests/unit/experiments/test_e2_counting.py -k enforce -q' — the test file is named after e2_counting.py but must test a function from run_e2_benchmark.py
findings.finding: The AC for the `emit_baseline` enforcement seam test (`pytest tests/unit/experiments/test_e2_counting.py -k enforce`) locates the test in `test_e2_counting.py`, but `emit_baseline` is defined in `run_e2_benchmark.py` — a separate module from `e2_counting.py`. The scope section defines `e2_counting.py` as containing only the two pure helpers (`classify_pair` and `run_validity`), while `emit_baseline` is explicitly placed in `run_e2_benchmark.py`. The test file name suggests it tests `e2_counting.py` module contents only, creating ambiguity about whether `emit_baseline` tests must import across module boundaries into `test_e2_counting.py` or belong in a separate file.
findings.location: ## Acceptance Criteria — AC 6 (emit_baseline seam); ## Scope (e2_counting.py and run_e2_benchmark.py)
scenarios: An implementer writes test_e2_counting.py to test only the pure helpers in e2_counting.py, placing emit_baseline tests in a separate file — but then `pytest test_e2_counting.py -k enforce` finds no tests and the AC proof command exits 0 with no tests run (pytest's default behavior for empty filter).
An implementer imports emit_baseline from run_e2_benchmark.py into test_e2_counting.py — works, but the file name is misleading and the import pulls in all of run_e2_benchmark's heavy dependencies.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add per-class construct-validity assertions (analogous to the class-5 tag-strip check in AC 1) that programmatically verify each class 1–4 mutation's semantic correctness — e.g., class-3 mutant's `children` text contains a named artifact reference AND its `children` list has no `depends_on`/`blocks` edge to the creating sibling; class-2 mutant's children partition by layer (no child spans >1 layer keyword); class-1 mutant's `plan_text` references a module name that is absent from `REAL_REPO`; class-4 mutant's `plan_text` contains a mechanism keyword not present in the control.
evidence: AC 1 prove command checks: `set(m)==set(ctl)=={'1','2','3','4','5'}`, counts ≥10, pairing, required fields, and class-5 tag strip — no semantic check for classes 1–4
Class-5 analogue: `assert all(v.get('control') and not v.get('mutant') for v in c5.values())` — this pattern exists only for class 5
No test in `tests/unit/experiments/test_e2_counting.py` or elsewhere covers mutation semantic correctness for classes 1–4
The `mutation_diff` field is committed but only for human inspection, not programmatic assertion
findings.finding: The mutation-assembly logic in `assemble_e2_mutants.py` for defect classes 1–4 has no per-class construct-validity test: AC 1's prove command only checks row counts, pairing, required fields, and the class-5 tag strip — it does NOT assert that class-1 mutations actually introduce an absent-capability assertion, that class-2 produces layer-partitioned (not vertically-sliced) children, that class-3 names an artifact without a `depends_on`/`blocks` edge, or that class-4 introduces an unnecessary mechanism. If the mutation logic is subtly wrong for any of these classes, the baseline silently measures the wrong defect, invalidating downstream R1/R3/R4 benchmarks.
findings.location: ## Acceptance Criteria — AC 1 prove command; `assemble_e2_mutants.py` scope entry; defect classes 1–4 mutation definitions in ## What
scenarios: Class-2 mutation accidentally produces a valid vertical slice (children still independently deliverable) — corpus is mislabeled, R3 detection rate is measured on non-layer-cake mutants
Class-3 mutation omits the artifact name in the child's text — the ordering-edge deletion is real but no consumption reference exists, making the defect un-flaggable by design
Class-1 mutation names a real module capability rather than an absent one — class-1 baseline is trivially low for the wrong reason
findings.suggested_fix: Extend `assemble_e2_mutants.py --dry-run --verify-clean` (AC 2) or add a dedicated `--verify-mutations` flag that, for each committed mutant row, runs a per-class semantic check function and exits non-zero naming the first failing pair. Mirror AC 1's class-5 prove pattern: for class 3, assert `any(edge in control_children for edge in ['depends_on','blocks']) and not any(edge in mutant_children ...)` etc. Add the four class checks to `test_e2_counting.py` or a new `test_e2_mutations.py` using synthetic corpus rows.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the baseline table's class-5 entry to use the actual registry/DET-floor id `P6` (not the Python function name `p6_ac_quality`), and clarify that DET-floor ids (`P1`–`P9`) are validated against `CANONICAL_DET`, not `registry.by_id()`.
evidence: Plan states: 'each validated against the live registry (`plan_review.registry.by_id`) at run time so no non-existent id is asserted'
Baseline table for class 5: 'p6_ac_quality (det_floor.py:686)'
Code confirms: `plan_review.registry.by_id()` contains LLM-layer ids (F1, E4, G1G2, G3, G4, etc.) — the DET floor functions are not registry entries
`src/rebar/llm/plan_review/registry.py:66` shows `CODEBASE_GROUNDED = frozenset({'E4', 'G1G2', 'A1', 'G6'})` — no `p6_ac_quality`
`src/rebar/llm/plan_review/registry.py:78` shows `CANONICAL_DET = ('P1', 'P2', ..., 'P9')` — the DET-floor id is `P6`, not `p6_ac_quality`
findings.finding: The baseline table references `p6_ac_quality` as a criterion id to be 'validated against the live registry (`plan_review.registry.by_id`) at run time' — but `p6_ac_quality` is a DET-floor Python function name (in `det_floor.py:686`), NOT a `registry.by_id()` key; the actual DET-floor registry id is `P6`. A runtime validation against `registry.by_id()` would fail to find `p6_ac_quality` and either raise a KeyError or silently omit it.
findings.location: ## What — baseline table row for class 5; gate-run mechanism description
scenarios: The harness's runtime registry validation rejects `p6_ac_quality` as an unknown id and either crashes or skips it, producing a silent gap in class-5 baseline attribution
A future implementer treats `p6_ac_quality` as a valid criterion id to pass to `classify_pair`, producing zero matches against findings whose `criteria` field contains `P6`
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `p6_ac_quality (det_floor.py:686)` with `P6` in the baseline table. Add a note that the runtime validation covers LLM-layer ids via `registry.by_id()` and DET-floor ids via `CANONICAL_DET` — these are separate namespaces.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a description of the `--self-check-isolation` flag to the Scope entry for `run_e2_benchmark.py`, specifying that it runs a single-pair gate call in the tmp clone and verifies the real store HEAD is unchanged before exiting.
evidence: Scope: 'run_e2_benchmark.py — benchmark harness (distinct deliverable). Clones … injects … runs … Applies classify_pair, journals … computes run_validity … emits e2_baseline.json …' — no mention of --self-check-isolation
AC7 prove: '.venv/bin/python docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/harnesses/run_e2_benchmark.py --self-check-isolation'
findings.finding: AC7's prove command calls `run_e2_benchmark.py --self-check-isolation`, but the Scope section's description of `run_e2_benchmark.py` never mentions a `--self-check-isolation` CLI flag. The flag's behavior (running a single-pair run to verify the real store HEAD is unchanged) is only implied by the AC prove command itself, leaving it as undescribed work.
findings.location: Scope (run_e2_benchmark.py description) vs AC7 prove command
scenarios: Implementer writes run_e2_benchmark.py per Scope with no --self-check-isolation flag; AC7 prove command fails
Implementer discovers the flag only from the AC, must infer its required behavior (run a single-pair isolation check) with no spec guidance
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope entry for run_e2_benchmark.py, add: '`--self-check-isolation`: runs a single mutant/control pair through the full mechanism and asserts the real `.tickets-tracker` HEAD is unchanged; exits 0 on success, non-zero on violation.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the false line-number citations `det_floor.py:345,392` (P2/P3 coverage-only probes) and `passes.py:269,514` (prompt loader / ISF summarizer) with the actual code locations where class-1/class-4 agent-tier code-grounding executes — i.e., the LLM AGENT-tier dispatch via `registry.AGENT_TIER` criteria `E4`, `A1`, `G6` routed through the workflow engine, and note that the grounding reads `cfg.repo_path` (set to `REAL_REPO_CHECKOUT` by `resolve_code_root`).
evidence: Plan text: 'the DET-floor absence probe (det_floor.py:345,392) and the agentic finders (passes.py:269,514) read the REAL modules — which is what makes class-1 (absent capability) and class-4 (unnecessary mechanism) grounding real'
det_floor.py:345 = `ev = grounding.refute_absence({"kind": "file", "name": ref}, repo_root=ctx.repo_root)` inside P2 `p2_resolution()` — coverage-only file-ref probe
det_floor.py:392 = `ev = grounding.refute_absence({"kind": "dependency", "name": pkg}, repo_root=ctx.repo_root or ".")` inside P3 `p3_package_existence()` — coverage-only package probe
passes.py:269 = `prompt = prompts.get_prompt(prompt_id, repo_root=cfg.repo_path)` inside `_resolve_system()` — prompt loader
passes.py:514 = `prompt = prompts.get_prompt("plan-review-isf-summarizer", repo_root=cfg.repo_path)` inside `summarize_for_isf()` — ISF log summarizer
registry.py:72-73: AGENT_TIER = frozenset({"G1G2", "E4", "A1", "G6", ...}) — the actual agent-tier criteria doing code grounding
findings.finding: The plan's gate-execution mechanism section asserts 'the DET-floor absence probe (`det_floor.py:345,392`) and the agentic finders (`passes.py:269,514`) read the REAL modules — which is what makes class-1 (absent capability) and class-4 (unnecessary mechanism) grounding real.' All four cited line numbers are wrong: `det_floor.py:345` is a `grounding.refute_absence()` call inside the coverage-only P2 file-reference probe (never blocks), `det_floor.py:392` is the P3 package-existence probe (also coverage-only, never blocks), `passes.py:269` is `prompts.get_prompt()` inside `_resolve_system()` (a prompt loader), and `passes.py:514` is `prompts.get_prompt()` inside `summarize_for_isf()` (an ISF log summarizer). None of these are the actual code-grounding mechanism for class-1 (absent-capability, E4-criterion) or class-4 (unnecessary-mechanism, A1/G6-criterion). The real code-grounding runs through the LLM AGENT tier (E4, A1, G6 in `registry.AGENT_TIER`), not at those DET-floor or prompt-loader lines.
findings.location: ## What — Gate-execution mechanism (verified — a NEW mechanism, not a harness reuse), bullet (b)
scenarios: A developer implementing `run_e2_benchmark.py` follows the cited line numbers to understand WHERE code-grounding is verified, finds only coverage-only probes and prompt loaders, and cannot validate that class-1/class-4 grounding is actually exercised.

If the harness is designed around the belief that P2/P3 DET probes are the absent-capability/unnecessary-mechanism detectors, the harness may misconfigure or mismeasure which gate paths it needs to exercise for class-1 and class-4 detection.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace bullet (b) in the gate-execution mechanism with: '(b) code-grounding = the real repo: `source="local"` causes `resolve_code_root(repo_root=REAL_REPO_CHECKOUT)` to set `ctx.repo_root` and `cfg.repo_path` to the real checkout; the AGENT-tier criteria (`registry.AGENT_TIER`: `E4`, `A1`, `G6`) each run a tool-using agent loop that reads the real modules via `cfg.repo_path` — this is what makes class-1 (absent capability, E4) and class-4 (unnecessary mechanism, A1/G6) grounding real. The DET-floor P2 (`det_floor.py:327-329`) abstains when `ctx.repo_root` is `None` — which never occurs here since the harness always supplies a real checkout root.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the `passes.py:269,514` citations in the gate-execution mechanism section with the correct line numbers for the agentic finder functions that execute code-grounding (e.g. `pass1_agent` or whichever pass1 variant runs the E4/G1G2 agent loops), so the claim that 'the agentic finders read the REAL modules' is traceable to actual agentic code.
evidence: passes.py:269 is `prompt = prompts.get_prompt(prompt_id, repo_root=cfg.repo_path)` inside `_prompt_for_plan()`, a shared prompt loader
passes.py:514 is `prompt = prompts.get_prompt('plan-review-isf-summarizer', repo_root=cfg.repo_path)` inside `_summarize_log_text()`
Neither function is an agentic finder; the agentic finder functions that actually execute code-grounding loops are elsewhere in passes.py (pass1_agent, etc.)
findings.finding: The plan cites `passes.py:269,514` as where 'the agentic finders (passes.py:269,514) read the REAL modules' to ground class-1 and class-4 mutants. Line 269 is inside `_prompt_for_plan()` — a prompt-loading helper — executing `prompts.get_prompt(prompt_id, repo_root=cfg.repo_path)`, and line 514 is inside `_summarize_log_text()` executing `prompts.get_prompt('plan-review-isf-summarizer', ...)`. Neither line is an agentic finder. The actual agentic finders (pass1_agent, the E4/G1G2/A1/G6 agent loops) are elsewhere in passes.py. The underlying claim that code grounding uses the real repo via cfg.repo_path is correct, but the specific line citations are misattributed.
findings.location: ## What — Gate-execution mechanism section: '(b) code-grounding = the real repo' bullet, citing `passes.py:269,514`
scenarios: An implementer wiring the harness from these citations finds prompt-loading helpers, not agentic finders, and cannot verify that class-1/class-4 grounding works as claimed
A reviewer checking 'does the grounding read the real repo?' cannot confirm this from the cited evidence

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the closed `mutation_op` enum vocabulary (e.g., `absent_capability | layer_cake_split | consumed_artifact_no_ordering | unnecessary_mechanism | untagged_operational_ac`) in the ## What section alongside the corpus schema.
evidence: AC1 prove command checks for the `mutation_op` key: `'mutation_op' + 'mutation_diff'` are listed as required mutant fields
The plan states each mutant carries a `mutation_op` but defines no closed enum for its values
The five defect class names are described in prose ('absent asserted capability', 'layer-cake split', etc.) but are never mapped to machine-readable `mutation_op` token values
ABSENCE: no `mutation_op` vocabulary table or enum definition appears anywhere in the plan
findings.finding: `mutation_op` field is required in every mutant corpus row (named in AC1's schema and prove command) but the plan never defines its closed value vocabulary — the implementer cannot know whether to write `'absent_capability'`, `'class_1'`, `'layer_cake_split'`, or any other token, and downstream consumers (R-story harnesses re-running the corpus) have no schema to validate against.
findings.location: ## What — corpus schema; AC1 prove command
scenarios: Two implementers independently write `assemble_e2_mutants.py` and produce incompatible `mutation_op` values, breaking corpus portability
An R-story harness that re-runs the corpus and filters by `mutation_op` silently matches zero rows because the token differs from what E2 wrote
findings.suggested_fix: Add a table mapping defect class numbers to their canonical `mutation_op` token strings, e.g.: class 1 → `absent_capability`, class 2 → `layer_cake_split`, class 3 → `consumed_artifact_no_ordering`, class 4 → `unnecessary_mechanism`, class 5 → `untagged_operational_ac`. Commit this as part of `e2_thresholds.json` or a top-level `e2_schema.json` so the AC prove command can validate it.

evidence: description is 25427 chars (> 24000)
findings.finding: Oversize signals suggest this unit may be too large for one session.
findings.suggested_fix: Split into smaller child tickets, each a coherent single outcome.

evidence: vague/subjective terms present: clean
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the concrete text-transformation procedure for class-1 mutations in `assemble_e2_mutants.py`'s spec — e.g., 'find the first import/`uses`/`depends on` module reference in the plan's description, append a sentence of the form `<module> exposes a <verb> method for <capability>`, where the capability string is drawn from a fixed vocabulary list committed alongside the harness.'
evidence: Plan class 1 definition: 'plan asserts an existing named module provides a capability it lacks'

Plan states mutations are 'mechanical' but provides no concrete text-transformation rule for class 1 (contrast: class 5 is 'STRIPS the [operator-attested] tag', class 3 is 'NO depends_on/blocks edge', class 2 is 'children re-partitioned by architectural layer')
Plan's own anti-circularity rule: 'no fresh LLM adjudication (which would duplicate the gate's own sidecar and be circular)'
findings.finding: Class 1 mutation ('absent asserted capability') is described as 'mechanical' but lacks a specified text-transformation procedure: the assembler must identify which named module in the base plan to falsely credit and which capability to claim it provides, which requires code-grounding knowledge not available from plan text alone. Classes 2/3/4/5 all have unambiguous text operations (re-partition children, delete edge, add change, strip tag). Class 1 does not, and an executing agent could either invoke an LLM (invalidated by the plan's own circular-adjudication rule) or make an arbitrary judgment call that reduces reproducibility.
findings.location: ## What — defect class 1 definition; ## Scope — assemble_e2_mutants.py description
scenarios: Executor of assemble_e2_mutants.py reaches class 1 mutation step and must decide: which sentence to add, which module name to insert, which nonexistent method to claim — with no algorithm specified
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sub-bullet under class 1 in ## What: 'Mechanical procedure: (a) extract the first named module/package reference from the base plan's `description` field using a regex `r\"(?:uses|imports|calls|relies on)\\s+([\\w.]+)\"` (or the plan's literal `depends_on` list); (b) append to the description a sentence from a fixed 10-entry capability-claim vocabulary file (`e2_class1_vocab.json`, committed with the harness), chosen deterministically by `hash(ticket_id) % len(vocab)`; (c) record the appended sentence as `mutation_diff`.' This makes the procedure fully mechanical and reproducible across implementors.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend the AC1 proving command to assert that every row contains all required keys (`pair_id`, `defect_class`, `role`, `source_ticket_id`, `plan_text`, `children`) and that every mutant row additionally contains `mutation_op` and `mutation_diff`.
evidence: AC1 states: 'every row carrying `pair_id`, `defect_class`, `role ∈ {mutant,control}`, `source_ticket_id`, `plan_text`, `children`, and (mutants) a `mutation_op` + `mutation_diff`'
The proving command contains no assertion checking `source_ticket_id`, `children`, `mutation_op`, or `mutation_diff` key presence in any row — only count/class/class-5-oa checks
Verify-command defect taxonomy item (4): fixture invalidity — the command does not actually exercise the full claim
findings.finding: AC1's proving command does not verify that every corpus row contains all required fields stated in the AC (`source_ticket_id`, `children`, and — for mutants — `mutation_op` and `mutation_diff`). The command only checks class counts, class-set equality, mutant/control count parity, and class-5 construct validity. A corpus with missing fields would pass AC1's command yet violate the AC's own stated schema requirement.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1 proving command
scenarios: An assembler script bug omits `children` from task-type rows or omits `mutation_op` from mutant rows; AC1's command passes; the downstream harness fails at runtime with a KeyError when reading those fields
findings.suggested_fix: Append to the python -c one-liner before `print('OK', dict(m))`: `required_all={'pair_id','defect_class','role','source_ticket_id','plan_text','children'}; required_mut=required_all|{'mutation_op','mutation_diff'}; bad=[r for r in rows if not((required_mut<=r.keys()) if r['role']=='mutant' else (required_all<=r.keys()))]; assert not bad, ('missing fields',bad[:2])`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the `e2_verdicts.jsonl` resume semantics explicitly: on startup the harness reads existing pair_ids from the file and skips already-completed pairs (idempotent append), and the AC for this file asserts each `pair_id` appears at most once (add a uniqueness check to the proving command).
evidence: 'journals raw verdicts incrementally (e2_verdicts.jsonl, crash-resumable)' — mechanism claimed but semantics not specified
Absence: no AC or prose describes what happens to e2_verdicts.jsonl on a restart — no mention of pair_id dedup, skip-if-exists logic, or overwrite policy
The AC for `e2_verdicts.jsonl` only checks that verdicts are in the closed vocabulary, not that each pair_id appears exactly once
findings.finding: The `e2_verdicts.jsonl` resume semantics on re-run are unspecified: when the harness is re-run after a partial crash, it is not stated whether it (a) appends to the existing file (risk: duplicate rows for already-completed pairs), (b) overwrites from scratch (loses crash-resume benefit), or (c) reads existing pair_ids and skips them. Without a defined deduplication or skip rule, a re-run can produce a verdict file with duplicate pair entries that invalidate the counting logic in `classify_pair` and `run_validity`.
findings.location: ## Scope — `run_e2_benchmark.py` ('journals raw verdicts incrementally (e2_verdicts.jsonl, crash-resumable)')
scenarios: Harness crashes after writing 60 of 100 rows; re-run appends the same 60 rows again plus 40 new ones; classify_pair now double-counts 60 pairs, inflating detection numbers
Operator re-runs to regenerate baseline after a code change; duplicate rows silently corrupt the baseline table without the AC catching it
findings.suggested_fix: On harness startup, load `{row['pair_id'] for row in existing_verdicts}` and skip any pair_id already present; open the file in append mode. Add to the AC prove command: `assert len(vs_ids := [json.loads(l)['pair_id'] for l in open('...e2_verdicts.jsonl')]) == len(set(vs_ids)), 'duplicate pair_ids'`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a runtime assertion in `run_e2_benchmark.py` that verifies REBAR_TRACKER_DIR redirection is actually honored before running the benchmark: e.g., inject a sentinel field into one clone ticket and assert the gate's PlanContext reads it (confirming the clone, not the real store, was used).
evidence: '`REBAR_TRACKER_DIR` redirects `assemble_context`\'s `show_ticket`/`list_tickets` to the tmp mutant store' (mechanism relies on config.py:126-128 precedence behavior)
'tracker precedence `config.py:119-136`' — cited as the internal mechanism, not a stable contract
Isolation AC only checks: `H0=$(git -C .tickets-tracker rev-parse HEAD)` … `[ "$H0" = "$H1" ]` — verifies git HEAD but not that reads were redirected to the clone
findings.finding: The harness isolates mutations via `REBAR_TRACKER_DIR` env-var redirection, coupling to specific internal implementation details cited by line number (`config.py:119-136`, `gate_dispatch.py:141-147`, `det_floor.py:345,392`). No interface contract stabilizes this coupling. If those internals change (e.g., tracker-dir precedence logic is refactored), the isolation mechanism silently fails — E2 produces results from the real store rather than the mutant clone — without triggering the isolation AC (which checks only git HEAD, not whether ticket reads were actually redirected to the clone).
findings.location: ## What — Gate-execution mechanism section; Scope `run_e2_benchmark.py`; AC 'ISOLATION-OK'
scenarios: A refactor of `config.py` tracker-dir resolution changes the precedence order; REBAR_TRACKER_DIR no longer wins; the harness runs the gate against real tickets silently; isolation AC still passes because git HEAD is unchanged; baseline results are computed from unmutated real plans.
findings.suggested_fix: In `run_e2_benchmark.py`, after cloning the store and injecting one sentinel mutation, call `rebar show <sentinel_id>` with REBAR_TRACKER_DIR set to the clone and assert the sentinel value is present. Fail fast with a clear error if the assertion fails. This makes the coupling self-testing rather than relying on line-number stability.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `TMP_MUTANT_STORE` is created as a `tempfile.mkdtemp()` (unique per invocation) and cleaned up via `try/finally` or `tempfile.TemporaryDirectory` context manager at harness exit, and add an AC assertion that no fixed tmp path is used.
evidence: 'clones `REAL_REPO/.tickets-tracker` → a tmp mutant store' — creation specified
Absence: no `tempfile.TemporaryDirectory` context manager, no `shutil.rmtree` on exit, no AC line verifying the tmp dir is removed post-run
The isolation AC only checks that the REAL store HEAD is unchanged — it does not verify the tmp dir was cleaned up
findings.finding: The `TMP_MUTANT_STORE` (a full clone of `.tickets-tracker`) has no defined cleanup/retirement path. The plan specifies creation (clone) and mutation (rebar edit) but never states when or how the tmp directory is removed — not in the harness description, not in an AC, and not in the isolation proof.
findings.location: ## Scope — `run_e2_benchmark.py`; ## What — Gate-execution mechanism ('clones REAL_REPO/.tickets-tracker → a tmp mutant store')
scenarios: Each benchmark re-run accumulates a full clone of the ticket store in /tmp or a fixed path; on a CI machine running repeated experiments, this accumulates unbounded disk usage
If the harness uses a fixed path (e.g., `/tmp/e2_mutant_store`) rather than a unique tmpdir, a second concurrent invocation corrupts the first's in-flight mutations
findings.suggested_fix: Use `with tempfile.TemporaryDirectory(prefix='e2_mutant_store_') as TMP_MUTANT_STORE:` in the harness `main()` so the clone is always cleaned up on exit (normal or exception).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `REBAR_SYNC_PULL=off` (alongside `REBAR_SYNC_PUSH=off`) to the harness's env setup for the tmp clone, so `ensure_fresh()` is disabled for all ticket reads against the ephemeral mutant store.
evidence: Plan specifies: 'os.environ["REBAR_TRACKER_DIR"] = TMP_MUTANT_STORE' and 'os.environ["REBAR_SYNC_PUSH"] = "off"' — no REBAR_SYNC_PULL=off
_reads.py:58: '_fresh(tracker)' is called unconditionally on every show_ticket()
reads.py:197: '_sync_disabled(os.path.dirname(os.path.realpath(tracker)))' reads load_config(tmp_dir) — a tmp dir has no rebar config, so ConfigError → returns False → sync not disabled
reads.py:216-226: The throttle marker is keyed by md5(clone_abs_path) — a fresh path means the marker doesn't exist (age=9999 > 60s) → reconverge proceeds on first call
REBAR_SYNC_PULL=off is confirmed as the correct knob (config.py:390: 'REBAR_NO_SYNC': ('sync', 'pull', 'REBAR_SYNC_PULL'))
findings.finding: The harness sets REBAR_SYNC_PUSH=off but does not set REBAR_SYNC_PULL=off. Every show_ticket() call against the tmp clone invokes ensure_fresh(), which uses a fresh md5-keyed throttle marker (never seen for the clone path) and will attempt a git fetch + reconverge of the clone against origin/tickets on the first read of each run. This adds unnecessary network I/O to every benchmark run, risks latency or failure in offline/CI environments, and could in edge cases merge new origin events into the clone mid-run (though union-merge semantics preserve injected EDIT events). The plan explicitly covers the outbound write risk (REBAR_SYNC_PUSH=off) but is silent on the inbound sync risk for the ephemeral clone.
findings.location: ## What — Gate-execution mechanism; run_e2_benchmark.py env setup block
scenarios: In a CI environment with network access, each benchmark run's first show_ticket() against the clone fetches from origin/tickets, adding network latency for 20–50 mutant+control pairs.
In an offline environment, git fetch fails silently (swallowed), but the reconverge attempt still occurs and may hold a lock briefly.
If origin has newer EDIT events for a base ticket between clone time and gate-run time, reconverge could add those events, potentially altering the ticket's reduced state after mutation injection.
findings.suggested_fix:
In run_e2_benchmark.py's gate-execution block, set both env vars before any gate call:
```python
os.environ["REBAR_TRACKER_DIR"] = TMP_MUTANT_STORE
os.environ["REBAR_SYNC_PUSH"] = "off"
os.environ["REBAR_SYNC_PULL"] = "off"  # prevent inbound sync on the ephemeral clone
```
Also add `REBAR_SYNC_PULL=off` to the mechanism block in the ## What description and the `--self-check-isolation` AC.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that the gate-run mechanism guarantees all ticket reads inside the gate honor the tmp clone: either (a) confirm no production gate code path uses PlanContext.tickets_root directly (all use rebar.config.tracker_dir() which honors REBAR_TRACKER_DIR), or (b) switch the harness to use source='attested' with a pre-populated snapshot + REBAR_TRACKER_DIR so use_tickets_root() is activated, or (c) explicitly call use_tickets_root(TMP_MUTANT_STORE) as a context manager wrapping the review_plan() call and document it in the mechanism section.
evidence: Plan claims: 'REBAR_TRACKER_DIR redirects assemble_context's show_ticket/list_tickets to the tmp mutant store'
gate_source.py:104-117: in local mode, gate_read_root() enters gate_session() only; it does NOT call use_tickets_root(), so current_tickets_root() stays None
orchestrator.py:208: tickets_root=current_tickets_root() — will be None in local mode regardless of REBAR_TRACKER_DIR
det_floor.py:109: 'tickets_root is materialized SEPARATELY (current_tickets_root() / cfg.tickets_path)' — suggesting some det-floor logic uses ctx.tickets_root, not env re-resolution
config.py:126-128: REBAR_TRACKER_DIR does win over repo_root in rebar.config.tracker_dir() — the _reads.show_ticket() path is sound; the gap is paths that use ctx.tickets_root directly
findings.finding: The plan asserts that setting REBAR_TRACKER_DIR redirects 'ALL ticket reads' inside the production gate to the tmp clone, but in local source mode gate_source.gate_read_root() does NOT activate use_tickets_root() (only attested mode does), so current_tickets_root() remains None and PlanContext.tickets_root is None. Any code path inside the gate that reads tickets via ctx.tickets_root directly — rather than re-resolving through rebar.config.tracker_dir() which honors REBAR_TRACKER_DIR — would bypass the isolation and read the real (unmutated) store, silently making the mutant and control identical for that path.
findings.location: ## What — Gate-execution mechanism section; specifically the bullet '(a) ticket content = the mutant: REBAR_TRACKER_DIR redirects assemble_context's show_ticket/list_tickets to the tmp mutant store'
scenarios: A det-floor check that reads ticket state via ctx.tickets_root (None) falls back to the live store — the mutant's seeded defect in the clone is invisible, collapsing mutant and control to identical gate inputs and making detection impossible for that check.
The harness isolation AC passes (HEAD unchanged) but the gate silently read the wrong store for some sub-checks, producing an invalid baseline.
findings.suggested_fix: In run_e2_benchmark.py, wrap the review_plan() call with an explicit use_tickets_root(TMP_MUTANT_STORE) context manager (imported from rebar.llm.config) so that current_tickets_root() returns the clone path for the duration of the run, matching what attested mode does — this guarantees PlanContext.tickets_root is populated from the clone regardless of source mode.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit prerequisite statement to the plan (either a `depends_on` edge to E1's ticket or a bolded note in ## What) specifying that `outcome_corpus.jsonl` must exist and satisfy the 9-key schema before E2's harnesses can run, and have `assemble_e2_mutants.py` emit a clear error message (not a stack trace) if the file is absent or schema-invalid.
evidence: Plan references 'outcome_corpus.jsonl, 628 rows, 9-key schema per runs/README.md:53-66' as an input, not a deliverable
No 'depends_on E1' or prerequisite statement appears anywhere in the plan

AC2's proving command (`assemble_e2_mutants.py --dry-run --verify-clean`) will fail with FileNotFoundError if the corpus is absent, but neither AC2 nor the README section specifies this as a prerequisite to check before running E2
findings.finding: The plan assumes `docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/runs/outcome_corpus.jsonl` (E1's output) already exists when `assemble_e2_mutants.py` runs, but no explicit prerequisite on E1's completion is stated in the plan. If E1 has not been completed (or its corpus is at a different path/schema), the assembler fails immediately with no actionable error from the AC's proving commands.
findings.location: ## What — 'Build a seeded-defect benchmark from real cleanly-closed plans drawn from the E1 outcome corpus'; ## Scope — assemble_e2_mutants.py description
scenarios: E2 is picked up before E1 is fully closed; executor runs AC2's proving command; script exits with FileNotFoundError on the corpus path; the error message gives no guidance that E1 must be completed first

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the plan (and in the e2_thresholds.json format description) whether `detection_min` is stored as an absolute integer or a float rate, and provide an explicit rule for how the harness handles the degraded-N case (e.g., 'if N<10, store detection_min as a float ≤1.0 and compare as rate; if N=10, store as integer').
evidence: Thresholds table: 'Class 5 | ≥7/10 flagged | ≤1/10 blocking-flagged'
Degradation ladder: 'drop to the available N≥5, keep the thresholds as RATES (≥70% detection, ≤10% blocking-FP)'
Class-5 ladder: '12 distinct clean bases ≥ 10, but it is tight … if fewer than 10 distinct suitable bases survive inspection … (b) run class 5 at the available N≥5'
AC3 schema check: 'assert all({"detection_min","blocking_fp_max"}<=set(t["classes"][k]) for k in t["classes"])'
AC4 checks 'meets_threshold' per class against the committed JSON
findings.finding: The pre-registered thresholds table pins class-5 detection at '≥7/10 flagged' (an absolute count), but the degradation ladder states that when a class cannot reach 10 real bases, thresholds should be kept as RATES ('≥70% detection, ≤10% blocking-FP'). If class 5 degrades to N=5 mutants (the ladder's stated minimum), ≥7/10 is literally impossible to achieve as an absolute count. AC4 checks `meets_threshold` against e2_thresholds.json, but the JSON schema specified in AC3 only requires `detection_min` and `blocking_fp_max` keys — it does not specify whether values are absolute counts or rates, leaving the degradation encoding undefined.
findings.location: What — pre-registered thresholds table (class 5 row) vs What — degradation ladder (class-5 section) vs AC3 JSON schema vs AC4 meets_threshold check
scenarios: Class-5 inspection yields only 7 suitable bases; 7 mutants are committed; e2_thresholds.json stores 7 (absolute); meets_threshold is unreachable unless the detector catches all 7 which equals 100% not ≥70%
Implementer stores 0.70 as a float rate in the JSON but the harness compares it to a detected integer count, causing a type error or wrong inequality

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief 'Harness pattern: running the gate in experiments' note to AGENTS.md (or a dedicated `docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/harnesses/README.md`) pointing to E2's verified REBAR_TRACKER_DIR mechanism so sibling experiment authors (E3–E6, R-stories) can adopt it consistently.
evidence: 'E2 introduces a new, verified gate-run mechanism' — plan explicitly names it as new
README.md AC only grep-checks for 'E2 — seeded-defect benchmark' and 'run-validity' and 'degrad' — no check that the gate-run mechanism pattern is findable by sibling experiment authors
Sibling experiments E3–E6 and R1–R4 all re-run the gate ('as R1/R3/R4/R2 each are') but have no pointer to E2's verified mechanism
findings.finding: The new env-var store-isolation pattern for running the production gate in experiment harnesses is a novel, non-obvious mechanism that future experiment authors (E3–E6, R-stories) will need to replicate. It is documented in README.md but absent from AGENTS.md or any developer-facing harness guide, reducing discoverability and risking inconsistent re-implementation in sibling experiments.
findings.location: ## What — Gate-execution mechanism; Scope `README.md` AC (grep checks)
scenarios: E3 author re-implements gate invocation differently (e.g., monkey-patches show_ticket), introducing an inconsistent isolation approach that yields different results than E2's baseline.
An AGENTS.md reader setting up a new experiment doesn't know about the REBAR_TRACKER_DIR pattern and invents an ad-hoc mechanism.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add retry/backoff (e.g., exponential backoff with ≥3 retries on 429/5xx) around each `review_plan()` call in `run_e2_benchmark.py`, and specify that a call exhausting all retries records the pair as `verdict=ERROR` (distinct from INDETERMINATE) so it is excluded from the validity count with a visible warning rather than silently dropped.
evidence: 'journals raw verdicts incrementally (e2_verdicts.jsonl, crash-resumable)' — stated for crash recovery but no per-call retry semantics
'review_plan(ticket_id, source="local", repo_root=REAL_REPO_CHECKOUT, sign=False, emit_sidecar=False, force=True)' — called per mutant/control with no error-handling wrapper described
Absence: no mention of retry, backoff, exponential delay, or Anthropic SDK error classes anywhere in harness spec
findings.finding: The harness calls `review_plan()` ~100+ times (5 classes × ≥10 mutants + 10 controls), each of which drives the Anthropic LLM API, but no retry/backoff logic for transient API failures (rate-limit 429, server 500, timeout) is specified anywhere in the plan or harness description. The 'crash-resumable' property of `e2_verdicts.jsonl` handles process crashes but not per-call API errors — a single 429 mid-run will abort the harness unless retried, requiring a full manual restart and potentially re-running already-completed pairs.
findings.location: ## Scope — `run_e2_benchmark.py`; ## What — Gate-execution mechanism
scenarios: Anthropic API returns 429 (rate limit) on the 47th of 100 calls — the harness aborts; operator must manually restart and hope the resume logic correctly skips completed pairs
Transient 500 error causes an exception that is neither retried nor recorded, so that pair's verdict is missing from e2_verdicts.jsonl entirely, silently shrinking the corpus
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap each `review_plan()` call in a helper that catches `anthropic.RateLimitError` / `anthropic.APIStatusError` with exponential backoff (e.g., tenacity `@retry(wait=wait_exponential(min=1, max=60), stop=stop_after_attempt(5))`); on final failure, write `{..., 'verdict': 'ERROR'}` to `e2_verdicts.jsonl` and treat ERROR rows as excluded (not as INDETERMINATE) so the validity guard still fires correctly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a cost estimate (approximate total API calls and expected dollar range) to the README/E2 writeup section, and consider a `--dry-run` mode for the benchmark harness that validates the corpus without executing gate calls, so authors can audit scope before spending.
evidence: 'agentic finders (passes.py:269,514)' — implies multi-turn LLM calls per gate invocation
At minimum 100 gate calls (5 classes × 10 mutants + 10 controls); each agentic pass may itself make multiple tool-use turns
Absence: no cost estimate, no token budget, no mention of model tier used for the benchmark run
findings.finding: The plan makes ~100+ LLM API calls (one `review_plan()` per mutant and control) with no cost estimate or budget guard. While the absolute number is small-scale, each gate call likely involves multiple LLM turns (the plan cites agentic finders at `passes.py:269,514`), making the per-call cost non-trivial and the total potentially surprising for a research experiment with no stated budget.
findings.location: ## What — Gate-execution mechanism; table of classes 1–4 with 'agentic' criteria
scenarios: Each agentic gate call makes 3–5 LLM turns; 100 calls × 4 turns = 400 API calls at Claude Sonnet pricing (~$0.015/1K output tokens) for a corpus that may produce hundreds of dollars of API spend without the author realizing it
A CI system triggers the benchmark repeatedly; unbounded cost accumulates

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace hardcoded `.venv/bin/python` and bare-bash shell prove commands in every AC with platform-neutral equivalents (e.g., `python -m pytest` with an activated venv, or `python -c "import subprocess, sys; ..."` for the git-ordering check) so ACs are runnable on Windows as well as POSIX platforms.
evidence: ".venv/bin/python -m pytest tests/unit/experiments/test_e2_counting.py -q" — hardcodes the POSIX virtualenv binary path
"H0=$(git -C .tickets-tracker rev-parse HEAD); ... H1=$(git -C .tickets-tracker rev-parse HEAD); [ \"$H0\" = \"$H1\"] && echo ISOLATION-OK" — uses bash $() substitution and POSIX [ ] test
"TH=$(git log --diff-filter=A --format=%H -1 -- ...); BL=$(git log ...); [ \"$TH\" != \"$BL\" ] && git merge-base --is-ancestor \"$TH\" \"$BL\" && echo PRE-REGISTERED-OK" — bash-only
On Windows the Python venv executable lives at .venv\Scripts\python.exe, not .venv/bin/python; $() and [ ] are not available in cmd.exe or PowerShell without bash
findings.finding: The AC prove commands hardcode POSIX-only shell syntax (`.venv/bin/python`, `$()` command substitution, `[ "$H0" = "$H1" ]` test brackets, `&&` short-circuit chaining) that are invalid on Windows — a supported platform/venue in the rebar matrix — making the ACs unrunnable as written on that platform.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — prove commands in every AC bullet (e.g., `.venv/bin/python -m pytest ...`, `H0=$(git -C .tickets-tracker rev-parse HEAD)`, `[ "$H0" = "$H1" ] && echo ISOLATION-OK`, `TH=$(git log --diff-filter=A ...)`)
scenarios: Windows platform (supported venue): running the isolation-check AC prove command in cmd.exe produces 'H0 is not recognized as an internal or external command' and the isolation guarantee is unverifiable; running the pytest prove command produces 'The system cannot find the path specified' because .venv/bin/python does not exist
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `.venv/bin/python -m pytest` with `python -m pytest` (caller activates venv or uses `py -m pytest` on Windows). For the git-ordering and isolation checks, extract them into a small Python helper script (e.g., `harnesses/verify_e2_acs.py`) that uses `subprocess.run(['git', ...])` and `pathlib` — callable as `python harnesses/verify_e2_acs.py --check isolation` on any platform — and reference that script in the ACs instead of the inline bash one-liners.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Formally define `target_criteria`'s type (e.g., `list[str]` of registry criterion ids) and add a per-class mapping table (class → `target_criteria` list) to the plan, committed in `e2_thresholds.json` so `classify_pair` and its tests have a single source of truth.
evidence: Plan defines `classify_pair(verdict_dict, target_criteria)` but gives no type annotation or value vocabulary for `target_criteria`
The baseline table names criteria per class in prose ('`E4`, `G1G2`, `G6` (agentic)') but these are never formally mapped to the `target_criteria` argument
The plan says 'a target-family finding surfaced with `decision ∈ {block, advisory}`' — but 'target-family' is defined only by reference to `target_criteria`, whose content is unspecified
No per-class `target_criteria` list is committed alongside `e2_thresholds.json` or elsewhere
findings.finding: The `classify_pair(verdict_dict, target_criteria)` helper's `target_criteria` parameter type and value vocabulary are never defined: the plan's baseline table lists criterion ids per class (e.g., `E4, G1G2, G6` for class 1) but never specifies whether `target_criteria` is a `list[str]` of registry criterion ids, a facet string, or something else — and the per-class values to pass are inferred from prose, not formally specified.
findings.location: ## What — 'AMBIGUOUS / INDETERMINATE verdict handling'; baseline table; Scope — `e2_counting.py`
scenarios: Implementer passes a facet string (`'codebase-grounding'`) when the harness expects criterion ids (`['E4', 'G1G2']`), causing all class-1 detections to count zero
The golden test for `classify_pair` cannot be written deterministically without knowing what `target_criteria` values are valid, leaving the test to use ad-hoc strings

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact JSON value format for `detection_min` and `blocking_fp_max` in `e2_thresholds.json` (e.g., always float fractions in `[0,1]`: `0.7`, `0.8`, `0.1`) and state that `meets_threshold` is computed as `detection_rate >= detection_min` where both are floats.
evidence: Threshold table mixes count notation ('≥7/10 flagged', '≥8/10 flagged') and rate notation ('≥80% / ≤10%')
Degradation ladder says 'keep the thresholds as RATES (≥70% detection, ≤10% blocking-FP)' when N<10, implying float fractions
AC3 prove command checks only `{'detection_min','blocking_fp_max'} <= set(t['classes'][k])` — not the value format

The harness must compare `detection_rate` from `e2_baseline.json` against `detection_min` from `e2_thresholds.json`, but cannot do so if one is a float (0.7) and the other is an integer (7) or string ('7/10')
findings.finding: The `detection_min` and `blocking_fp_max` values in `e2_thresholds.json` are specified in the plan as counts (≥7/10, ≥8/10) and fractions (≥70%, ≤10%) interchangeably, but the JSON field format (float fraction 0.7 vs integer count 7 vs string '7/10') is never defined — creating an ambiguity for both the writer of `e2_thresholds.json` and the harness logic that reads and applies these values.
findings.location: ## What — pre-registered thresholds table; AC3 prove command
scenarios: Implementer writes `detection_min: 7` (count), harness computes `detection_rate: 0.7` (float), comparison `0.7 >= 7` is always False — every class falsely reports not meeting threshold
Degradation-ladder path (N=8) stores `detection_min: 0.7` while the normal path stores `detection_min: 7`, making the `meets_threshold` logic inconsistent between the two paths
findings.suggested_fix: Pin both fields as float fractions in `[0,1]` (e.g., `detection_min: 0.7`, `blocking_fp_max: 0.1`). Update the threshold table's prose to use decimal notation consistently. Add a sentence to AC3 that the prove command also validates `0.0 <= t['classes'][k]['detection_min'] <= 1.0`.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
